title: A Novel Hadronic Calorimeter With A Direct Neutron Readout
abstract: A neutron-sensitive sampling calorimeter based on alternating lead absorber and gadolinium-loaded liquid scintillator layers is investigated using detailed Geant4 Monte Carlo simulations. In addition to the conventional prompt calorimetric signal, the proposed detector records delayed energy from neutron moderation and capture, providing direct information on the neutron component of hadronic showers. A six-layer Pb/LAB-Gd calorimeter exposed to 10~GeV protons is studied to characterize its neutron response and evaluate its impact on calorimetric performance. The delayed deposited energy is found to be almost perfectly proportional to the neutron-capture multiplicity, providing a direct calibration of the neutron-sensitive signal. Event-by-event analyses further reveal a clear relationship between the prompt calorimetric response and the delayed neutron observable, demonstrating that the latter contains substantial information on the invisible hadronic energy. Exploiting this correlation through a simple nonlinear event-by-event correction improves the prompt-energy resolution from 21.8% to 13.3% without rejecting events. Furthermore, the analysis of events with similar neutron multiplicities indicates that neutron-production fluctuations constitute a major contribution to the overall hadronic energy resolution. These results demonstrate the potential of gadolinium-loaded sampling calorimeters to recover part of the invisible hadronic energy and significantly improve hadronic energy reconstruction and energy resolution.

bite.hook: A new gadolinium-loaded calorimeter improves hadronic energy resolution from 21.8% to 13.3% by capturing neutrons.
tldr: They simulated a novel sampling calorimeter that pairs lead absorbers with gadolinium-loaded liquid scintillators to detect delayed neutron signals.
Using Geant4 Monte Carlo simulations, they exposed a six-layer calorimeter model to 10 GeV protons to track prompt and delayed energy deposits.
They found that delayed energy correlates with neutron capture, allowing a simple correction that drastically improves energy resolution.
bite.whyItMatters: Measuring the energy of subatomic particles is notoriously difficult because a large fraction of their energy is lost to 'invisible' nuclear processes. This design offers a practical way to recover that lost information, potentially upgrading the precision of future particle colliders.
bite.eli5: Imagine trying to weigh a box of bouncy balls by listening to them hit the floor. Some balls bounce immediately, but others get trapped and rattle around for a while before stopping. By listening to those late, delayed rattles (like detecting delayed neutrons), we get a much more accurate measurement of how many balls were in the box.

keyNumbers.label: Proton energy
keyNumbers.context: The energy of the proton beam used in the Geant4 simulations to test the calorimeter.

keyNumbers.label: Uncorrected energy resolution
keyNumbers.context: The baseline prompt-energy resolution of the calorimeter before applying the neutron-capture correction.

keyNumbers.label: Corrected energy resolution
keyNumbers.context: The improved energy resolution achieved after applying a simple nonlinear event-by-event correction.

glossary.shortDef: An instrument that measures the energy of hadrons by absorbing them and detecting the resulting particle showers.
glossary.eli5Def: A giant, dense block used in physics labs to stop fast-moving particles and measure how much energy they had.

glossary.shortDef: A liquid that flashes light when hit by radiation, enhanced with gadolinium to highly attract and capture neutrons.
glossary.eli5Def: A special glowing liquid mixed with a metal that acts like a magnet for stray neutrons, making them easy to spot.

glossary.shortDef: A software toolkit used to simulate the passage of particles through matter using Monte Carlo methods.
glossary.eli5Def: A highly accurate physics simulator program used by scientists to predict how particles will behave inside a detector.

glossary.shortDef: The process by which fast neutrons slow down through collisions with light atomic nuclei.
glossary.eli5Def: Bumping fast-moving neutrons into other atoms to slow them down, like a pinball losing speed as it hits bumpers.
